likely_position: Counsel would most likely avoid contesting the core San Jose point and instead assume, for risk-management purposes, that at least some of the Signal content (franchise terms, RFP timing) qualifies as a "public record" under the content/context test of City of San Jose v. Superior Court (2017) 2 Cal.5th 608, since it plainly relates to "the conduct of the public's business." Counsel would likely concede that the clerk's search was legally inadequate — San Jose requires an inquiry to individual officials about potentially responsive material on personal accounts/devices, not just a search of city systems — and would recommend promptly curing this by directing councilmembers to search personal devices, producing the 40 surviving screenshots (redacted as needed for privilege/deliberative process claims that counsel would likely assert are weak but worth raising), and issuing a supplemental CPRA response rather than defending the "no responsive records" answer through litigation. On the deleted messages, counsel would resist framing auto-deletion as a knowing/willful § 34090 violation (arguing the retention schedule's applicability to individual officials' personal-device ephemeral messaging is legally unsettled and the councilmembers' conduct was not city action), while still opening a litigation hold now, quietly documenting good faith, and treating any adverse-inference or spoliation argument in the franchise litigation as a settlement-pressure factor rather than something to fight to a published ruling. Counsel would likely recommend settling/mooting the CPRA suit (supplemental production, stipulated declaratory relief, negotiated fees under Gov. Code § 7923.115) rather than litigating San Jose's scope to judgment, refer the three-councilmember group chat for a quiet internal Brown Act compliance review (without volunteering a serial-meeting admission), and adopt going forward a personal-device/ephemeral-messaging and records-retention policy — all aimed at containing exposure and optics rather than vindicating the city's original "no responsive records" position.
